front: What are Metrics in UI/UX design?
back: Metrics are quantitative measurements that quantify user behavior and product performance, providing objective data about how users interact with a digital product.

front: What are Targets in UI/UX design?
back: Targets are specific, measurable benchmarks that define what success looks like for a product, guiding design decisions and evaluating performance.

front: What does HEART stand for in UX metrics?
back: HEART stands for Happiness (user satisfaction), Engagement (level of interaction), Adoption (new user acquisition), Retention (user return rate), and Task success (effectiveness).
The percentage of users who complete a desired action (like purchasing, signing up) out of the total number of visitors.
The percentage of visitors who leave the website after viewing only one page without taking any action.
The percentage of users who successfully complete a specific task, measuring the effectiveness of the interface in helping users achieve goals.
System Usability Scale - a standardized 10-question survey used to measure perceived usability, yielding a score from 0-100.
Net Promoter Score - measures user loyalty by asking how likely users are to recommend the product on a scale of 0-10.

front: What are SMART goals in UI/UX?
back: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound goals that provide clear direction for design projects.
A method comparing two versions of a webpage to determine which performs better by showing each version to different user groups.
The average time taken by users to complete a specific task, measuring efficiency of the interface.
The percentage of users who make errors while completing tasks, indicating usability issues in the design.
A metric measuring user interaction including clicks, shares, comments, and time spent on content.
The percentage of users who stop using a product over a specific period, indicating retention problems.

front: Why are metrics important in UX design?
back: Metrics provide objective data to identify usability issues, measure design improvements, justify design decisions, and align design with business goals.
